coredata.dc:title: Sulfhydryl modified carbon dots as dual mode fluorescent and colorimetric probes for sensitive acrolein detection
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiangbin
Yang, Wei
Liu, Yuanfa
coredata.dc:description: The development of efficient detection method for acrolein (ACR) is imperative due to its ingestion can cause a serious threat to human health. Herein, a novel carbon dots fluorescence/colorimetric dual-mode probe (SH-CDs) for the detection of ACR was developed by using o-phenylenediamine as precursors and thiol groups (-SH) as a post-modified functional group. The yellow fluorescence of SH-CDs can be effectively quenched in the presence of ACR, resulting in a distinct gray appearance under sunlight. The dual-mode probe exhibited exceptional selectivity and sensitivity employing the fluorimetric/colorimetric mode, achieving a limit of detection at 0.31 μM and 0.49 μM respectively. The practicality and reliability of the SH-CDs probe were confirmed by detecting ACR in vegetable oil with favorable recoveries (94.0–112.0%). Additionally, a portable monitoring platform was constructed by integrating the probe with smartphone-assisted colorimetric reader. This work provided a novel perspective for rapid and efficient detection of ACR in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acrolein
Dual-mode probe
Visual detection
DFT calculation
Smartphones-assisted device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ACR based on smartphone-sensing platform
Application for detection in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key R&D Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFD2100300) and Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food (BM2020023) as well as Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the SH-CDs. (b) HR-TEM image of SH-CDs. (c) XRD pattern of the SH-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of the SH-CDs. (e) XPS full spectrum of SH-CDs. High resolution XPS spectra and the corresponding fitting curves of (f) C 1s, (g) N 1s and (h) B 1s of SH-CDs.

(a) The UV–vis and fluorescence profiles of SH-CDs (Inset: the photos under natural and UV light). (b)The fluorescence behavior of SH-CDs under different excitation signals. (c) The emission spectra of SH-CDs before and after addition ACR (Inset: the photos under UV light). (d) The UV–vis signal of SH-CDs before and after addition ACR (Inset: the photos under natural light).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of SH-CDs probe adding various concentrations of ACR (0–100 μМ), the inset: the corresponding fluorescence images at different concentrations of ACR (λex = 365 nm). (b) The related data calibration curve, the inset: linear fitting curve between I500 and lnCACR. (c) UV–vis absoprtion of SH-CDs probe adding various concentrations of ACR (0.5–100 μМ), the inset: the corresponding colorimetry images at different concentrations of ACR. (d) The corresponding data calibration curve, the inset: linear fitting curve between A360 and lnCACR.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of ACR and fluorescence excitation and emission of the SH-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of SH-CDs before and after adding ACR. (c) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of SH-CDs before and after adding ACR. (d) Calculated HOMO/LUMO distribution of SH-CDs before and after adding ACR.

(a, b) Selectivity study of SH-CDs dual-mode probe for recognizing ACR and its structural analogs; (c, d) Study on the anti-interference ability of the SH-CDs dual-mode probe against different metal ions and potential interferents.

(a) Schematic diagram illustrating the visual detection procedure for ACR integrated with a smartphone-assisted readout platform. (b) The schematic of TCH detection using a smartphone; (c) The standard curve between lnCACR and R/(R + G + B) obtained through smartphone visualization under fluorescent mode. (d) The standard curve between lnCACR and green channel value obtained through smartphone visualization under sunlight.
Illustration of SH-CDs development and its application for colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode ACR analysis.
The determination of ACR spiked in real samples using dual-mode probe (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Sulfhydryl modified carbon dots as dual mode fluorescent and colorimetric probes for sensitive acrolein detection

State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center for Functional Food, National Engineering Laboratory for Cereal Fermentation Technology, Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province, Jiangnan University, 1800 Lihu Road, Wuxi, 214122, Jiangsu, People's Republic of China.

National Engineering Research Center for Functional Food
National Engineering Laboratory for Cereal Fermentation Technology
Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of efficient detection method for acrolein (ACR) is imperative due to its ingestion can cause a serious threat to human health. Herein, a novel carbon dots fluorescence/colorimetric dual-mode probe (SH-CDs) for the detection of ACR was developed by using o-phenylenediamine as precursors and thiol groups (-SH) as a post-modified functional group. The yellow fluorescence of SH-CDs can be effectively quenched in the presence of ACR, resulting in a distinct gray appearance under sunlight. The dual-mode probe exhibited exceptional selectivity and sensitivity employing the fluorimetric/colorimetric mode, achieving a limit of detection at 0.31 μM and 0.49 μM respectively. The practicality and reliability of the SH-CDs probe were confirmed by detecting ACR in vegetable oil with favorable recoveries (94.0–112.0%). Additionally, a portable monitoring platform was constructed by integrating the probe with smartphone-assisted colorimetric reader. This work provided a novel perspective for rapid and efficient detection of ACR in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acrolein (ACR), a volatile and extremely harmful aldehyde resulting from the oxidation of lipids in edible oils, has a significant impact on the quality and safety of the oil (Grosso et al., 2024). Moreover, it is an infamous pollutant that is predominantly produced by sources such as wildfires, vehicles emissions, and fuel combustion (K. Jiang, Huang, et al., 2022). The ingestion of ACR has been associated with potential health hazards. Specifically, the accumulation of ACR in the human body has frequently been linked to a range of chronic ailments, including but not limited to cardiovascular diseases, diabetes mellitus, various forms of cancer, and Alzheimer's disease (K. Jiang, Huang, et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022). Dietary intake serves as the primary source of ACR for humans, and it has been detected in a diverse range of food items, including cheese, donuts, bread, potatoes, and fish (Henning et al., 2017). According to the World Health Organization (WHO), it is recommended that the Tolerable Daily Intake (TDI) of ACR should not exceed 7.5 μg/kg. However, the observed maximum exposure to ACR exceeds significantly the established WHO standards, with an approximate value of 0.1 mg/kg/day (D. Zhang et al., 2020). Therefore, a quantitative assessment of ACR levels in food and the environment is imperative for mitigating potential health risks.

To date, various approaches have been developed for the identification of ACR, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Kumar et al., 2022), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Custodio-Mendoza et al., 2022), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) as well the detection for ACR metabolites (Tong et al., 2022). These methods exhibit exceptional precision and reliable consistency. However, the practical application of real-time and fast detection of ACR is limited due to the high cost of instruments and the complexity of sample preparation processes. Recently, the fast response and sensitive nature of fluorimetry, along with the simple operation and visualizability of colorimetry, have garnered significant attention for both methods (Chen et al., 2024). Based on these, several fluorescence and colorimetry probes have been developed for the quantification of ACR (Choi et al., 2023; K. Jiang, Huang, et al., 2022; H. Xu et al., 2022). However, these traditional single signals are susceptible to external interference, thereby impeding their further advancement towards practicality. (L. Wang et al., 2024). On the contrary, the proposition of dual-signal response technologies aims to amalgamate the advantages of both colorimetric and fluorescent analysis. By utilizing dual output signals as an internal calibration, this approach reduces signal fluctuations and yields more compelling experimental results. Additionally, the integration of these two modes can further facilitate the construction of dual-mode probe, endowed with the capability to autonomously select the most suitable detection mode based on actual requirements and circumstances (Xiao et al., 2024).

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials, have garnered significant attention in the field of fluorescence sensing due to their tunable fluorescence, remarkable stability, and easy surface functionalization (J. Xu et al., 2023; Chelladurai et al., 2024). In the past decades, a diverse range of carbon sources including small molecules, polymers, and biomass have been selected for the synthesis of CDs (Y. Jiang et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024). During the synthesis process, these precursor substances undergo a transformation that leading to the formation of CDs with diverse functional groups (Alafeef et al., 2024). However, there is currently no available literature that discusses the utilization of a smartphone-assisted fluorescence and even multi-mode CDs probe for the visual detection of ACR concentrations.

Herein, a CDs based dual-mode probe (SH-CDs) was developed for the rapid and visual detection of ACR in foodstuffs using fluorescence and colorimetric methods. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the novel sensor with exceptional optical properties was developed by utilizing o-phenylenediamine as a precursor and sulfhydryl as a post-modifier functional group. The prepared SH-CDs demonstrated remarkable sensitivity and precision in determining ACR through both colorimetric and fluorescent methods. Upon the introduction of ACR, the yellow fluorescence of SH-CDs can be effectively quenched, which can be attributed to the static quenching effect (SQE). Meanwhile, a noticeable color change from pale yellow to gray under sunlight was observed. More significantly, the integration of a smartphone enhanced the visual and portable detection capabilities of the dual-mode probe for ACR. Additionally, the dual-mode probe successfully accomplished the real-time detection of ACR in vegetable oil with outstanding sensitivity, selectivity, and feasibility. Therefore, this dual-mode strategy presents an innovative approach for the sensitive rapid monitoring of ACR in food.

Acrolein (ACR), formaldehyde (FA), acetaldehyde (AA), methylacet aldehyde (MA) glyoxal (GO), methylglyoxal (MGO), Na₂SO₄, K₃PO₄, NaCl, CaCl2, KCl, and FeCl3 were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). Glutamic acid (Glu), serine (Ser), oleic acid (OA), linoleic acid (LA), α-tocopherol (α-Toc), and β-carotene (β-Car) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Ethanol, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), o-phenylenediamine (oPD), 5-bromosalicylaldehyde (BrSA), concentrated H2SO4 (98 wt%), concentrated HCl (38 wt%), Rhodamine B, and sodium sulphide nonahydrate were were obtained from Titan Technology Co., (Shanghai, China). The corn oil and peanut oil were purchased from a nearby market. All chemical reagents utilized in this research were of analytical grade and remained unaltered in their initial condition.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected from a JEM-ARM200F field-emission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The UV–visible absorption spectra were acquired with UV-1750 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fluorescence spectra were obtained from a HITACHI F-7000 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were scanned using a Vertex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Thermo Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used for providing X-ray optoelectronic energy spectrum (XPS) information. A D8 ADVANCE (XRD, Bruker, Germany) was used to conduct the X-ray diffractometer. The LifeSpec II Spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) was employed to carry out the fluorescent lifetime analysis.

The preparation of CDs derived oPD (oPD-CDs) was successfully performed by solvothermal treatment. Briefly, 0.11 g oPD and 1 mL H2SO4 were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. After 30 min of sonication, the mixtures were then transferred to a 25 mL stainless autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The crude product was naturally cooled to room temperature, and then was purified via a dialysis bag (500 Da) to remove unreacted precursors and impurities. Subsequently, a mixture of oPD-CDs (25 mg), BrSA (110 mg), and ethanol (20 mL) was introduced into a 50 mL round-bottomed flask, followed by refluxing at 85 °C for 2 h to obtain the bromine atom doped CDs (Br-CDs). Finally, the thiol group was modified to the Br-CDs using sodium sulfide according to the previously reported method with some modifications (L. Zhang et al., 2016). At room temperature, Br-CDs (0.44 mmol) and sodium sulphide nonahydrate (2.5 mmol) were individually dissolved in a sealed tube containing 5 mL DMF and then was stirred for 24 h. Subsequently, the solution was diluted with 20 mL of water and the pH was adjusted to approximately 2.0 with HCl solution. The resulting precipitate was then subjected to centrifugal washing and dried at 50 °C to obtain SH-CDs.

The SH-CDs (10 μL, 2 mg/mL) solution was mixed with 30 μL of the ACR in a centrifuge tube, and then diluting the solution to 1 mL with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at pH = 7.0. The fluorescence spectra were then recorded after incubating at room temperature for 40 min. The standard curve was established in order to facilitate the quantitative analysis of ACR by correlating the fluorescence intensity at 500 nm with its concentration. The fluorescence images of different concentration of ACR were acquired on a smartphone under 365 nm UV light and subsequently converted into corresponding RGB values using color detection software. Then, a correlation was established between the logarithm of ACR concentration and the R/(R + G + B) ratio. The images were acquired under conditions of darkness to mitigate potential interference from environmental light sources. To standardize the image capture process, all images were taken under consistent lighting conditions, with a fixed distance from the UV light source, and from the same angle.

Additionally, the absorption spectra of the probe were acquired using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, while simultaneously capturing images of the detection system under sunlight with varying concentrations of ACR using a smartphone.

The capability of SH-CDs to detect ACR in real samples was examined by employing peanut oil and corn oil. The ACR concentrations of 0.5, 5, and 20 μМ were subsequently introduced into the collected real oil samples to conduct the recovery test. Subsequently, a solution was prepared by combining 0.5 mL of vegetable oil with 2 mL of ethanol and exposing it to ultrasound for 30 min. The resulting emulsion was centrifuged at a speed of 8000 r/min for 10 min, followed by filtration of the supernatant through a nitrocellulose membrane with 0.22 μm pore. The fluorescence/colorimetric analysis of the extraction solutions was conducted in accordance with the methodology outlined in Section 2.3 and 2.4, respectively. The identification of samples containing spikes was also accomplished by employing the SH-CDs probe in conjunction with a smartphone-assisted sensing platform.

The crucial step in the synthesis of SH-CDs involved the precise functionalization of CDs with surface sulfhydryl (SH)-type ligands. The design of the dual-mode probe SH-CDs was based on the formation of a Michael adduct through trapping ACR with -SH groups. Here, the precursor o-phenylenediamine (oPD) was utilized to synthesize oPD-CDs with plentiful amino groups on the surface under acidic conditions (Fig. S1). The SH-CDs were then obtained through a two-step method, which involved Schiff base reactions and nucleophilic substitution reactions, to modify the oPD-CDs with the -SH functional group. The morphology of SH-CDs was examined through TEM and High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). As presented in Fig. 1 a, the synthesized SH-CDs exhibited an obvious transformation and were assembled into a lamellar structure compared to oPD-CDs (Fig. S1a). In the HRTEM image (Fig. 1b), the SH-CDs displayed distinct lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which can be attributed to the (100) plane of graphite (Hu et al., 2022). The crystal characteristic of SH-CDs was explored by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The X-ray diffraction pattern displayed broad peaks at approximately 2θ = 21.2° without any presence of impurity peaks (Fig. 1c). This particular peak corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating that the SH-CDs possess an amorphous carbon state (X. Xu, Wang, et al., 2021). The chemical structure and functional group of the SH-CDs were analyzed through FT-IR technology. The vibrational peaks of the C-H group were observed at 2927 and 2856 cm−1, as illustrated in Fig. 1d, while the obvious absorption band appeared at 1653 cm−1 should be assigned to C=O stretching (J. Zhang et al., 2023). Moreover, the absorption peaks observed at 3278 and 1533 cm−1 can be attributed to the presence of the N-H functional group, providing evidence for the abundant amine groups present on the surface of SH-CDs (L. Wang et al., 2024). The characteristic peaks at 1340 cm−1 and 1035 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-N and C-O, respectively (Ai et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2024). In addition to, the SH-CDs exhibited two characteristic peaks at 2605 and 1211 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching modes of -SH and C-S groups (Liao et al., 2018; Zhan et al., 2023). The comparison with Fig. S1c suggested that the surface modification of oPD-CDs with -SH has been successfully achieved. Meanwhile, XPS spectra were also acquired to validate the successful synthesis of SH-CDs as depicted in Fig. 1e–h. Compared to oPD-CDs (Fig. S1d), SH-CDs exhibited a distinct peak at 163.5 eV corresponding to S 2p (Das & Mohapatra, 2017), in addition to the peaks of C1s, N1s, and O1s observed in the wide-scan spectrum. Furthermore, the high-resolution C1s spectrum of SH-CDs confirmed the presence of -COOH (289.3 eV), C=O (288.5 eV), C-N (286.9 eV), C-S (285.8 eV), and C-C (284.8 eV) (L. Wang et al., 2024; Yuan et al., 2020). Regarding the N 1s spectrum, two peaks at 399.5 and 398.2 eV were assigned to C=N-C and C-N-C bonds, respectively (Fan et al., 2024). The result was in accordance with the FT-IR analysis, indicating the formation of Schiff base within SH-CDs. Significantly, the high-resolution analysis of S 2p in SH-CDs displayed two distinct binding states, corresponding to C-S (164.2 eV) and S-H (163.1 eV) (Copur et al., 2019). The results demonstrated successful modification of -SH functional groups on the surface of SH-CDs, which provided active sites for coordination with ACR.

The optical properties of synthesized SH-CDs were extensively investigated through UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2 a). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of SH-CDs exhibited two distinct absorption peaks at 274 nm and 346 nm, which were attributed to the π - π∗ transition of C=C/C=N bond and n - π∗ transition of C=O, respectively (Mandal et al., 2020; Park et al., 2016). The SH-CDs solution exhibited a vivid yellow luminescence when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp, as depicted in the inset of Fig. 2a. The maximum excitation wavelength of SH-CDs was observed at around 415 nm, along with the optimal emission signal at 500 nm. As shown in Fig. 2b, the emission signal position remained unchanged when the excitation wavelength varied from 365 to 435 nm, suggesting the excitation-independent luminescent property of SH-CDs. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of SH-CDs was determined to be 26.35% by comparing it with the reference Rhodamine B (with a QY of 69%), indicating the remarkable potential of SH-CDs as an effective signal indicator. Furthermore, there was no noticeable change in the fluorescence emission even after being continuously exposed to 365 nm irradiation for 60 min, which indicated the superior photobleaching resistance of SH-CDs (Fig. S2a). After a storage period of 30 days, the luminescence intensity of SH-CDs remained almost constant in comparison to the initial spectra, indicating excellent stability during storage (Fig. S2b). These remarkable characters make them highly suitable for serving as excellent fluorescent probes.

Interestingly, the addition of ACR to the sensing system resulted in a noticeable reduction in the emission intensity of SH-CDs, accompanied by the quenching of their yellow fluorescence (Fig. 2c). Meanwhile, a noticeable transition in color from pale yellow to gray and an increment in absorbance were observed in the system solution (Fig. 2d). Therefore, both color variations offered a viable approach for achieving synchronized colorimetric and fluorescent detection of ACR. Furthermore, we investigated the fluorescence and absorbance response of SH-CDs towards ACR at different incubation times to optimize their sensing performance. As shown in Fig. S3, both the fluorescence response and absorbance reached stability after an incubation period of 40 min. Therefore, a fluorimetric/colorimetric reaction time of 40 min was deemed appropriate for subsequent analysis. Subsequently, the influence of pH values on the detection signals of the SH-CDs dual-mode probe was examined. As depicted in Fig. S4, the fluorescence and absorbance signals exhibited a pronounced increase or decrease upon pH adjustment to 9.0, indicating a significant reduction in probe sensitivity. In contrast, the dual channels exhibited relatively high sensitivity and stability within the pH range of 3.0–7.0, prompting the selection of a pH 7.0 test solution for ACR measurements.

The quantitative analysis of ACR was conducted using both colorimetric and fluorescence methods to assess the dual-mode sensing capabilities of SH-CDs. Firstly, the fluorescence mode was utilized for the monitoring of ACR under optimal experimental conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence peak of SH-CDs at 500 nm gradually decreased with increasing concentrations of ACR, concomitant with the quenching of monochromatic yellow fluorescence. The correlation between the fluorescence intensity at 500 nm and the logarithm of ACR concentration was found to be highly linear, fitting well with the equation: I500 = −380.43 lnCACR + 2197.5 (R2 = 0.992), covering a concentration range from 0.5 to 100 μМ (Fig. 3b). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.31 μМ using formula 3δ/K, where δ represents the standard deviation of the blank and K denotes the slope of calibration curve.

During the process of colorimetry analysis, the UV–vis absorption signal of the probe gradually increased at 360 nm with increasing ACR concentration, and gray substances were produced (Fig. 3c). The absorbance showed a linear increase in response to variations in the logarithm of ACR concentration. This correlation can be mathematically represented as: A360 = 0.0802 ln CACR + 0.3052 (R2 = 0.993), within the range of 0.5–100 μМ (Fig. 3d). The LOD of the colorimetric method can reach as low as 0.49 μМ, demonstrating that the sensor also has promising capabilities in colorimetry mode for precise detection of ACR. Based on the information provided in Table S1, the dual-mode probe in this study exhibited similar or better outcomes compared to previously reported probes in terms of detection sensitivity and linear range. Specifically, the fluorescence detection limit of our SH-CDs probe (0.31 μM) is comparable to or better than most single-mode probes, such as the naphthalimide-based probe (0.58 μM) and the dicyanoisophorone-based probe (0.56 μM). Furthermore, the colorimetric detection limit of our probe (0.49 μM) also demonstrates an improvement over other colorimetric methods. Notably, the mutual validation offered by dual signal output could enhance the accuracy of ACR detection and mitigate unreliable result caused by using a single optical detection method. The color changes of the dual-mode probe can be readily observed under ultraviolet light and sunlight, facilitating effortless visual identification of ACR.

Given the remarkable performance of SH-CDs in dual-mode detection of ACR through fluorimetric and colorimetric techniques, it was imperative to investigate its plausible sensing mechanism. Frequently observed mechanisms for quenching include fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), static quenching effect (SQE), and the inner filter effect (IFE) (J. Wang et al., 2021). The absorption spectrum of ACR was initially explored, in addition to the analysis of excitation and emission spectra of SH-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the UV–vis absorption band of ACR displayed no overlap with the emission peak of SH-CDs, thereby excluding the probability of IFE or FRET occurring within the SH-CDs@ACR sensing process (Tang et al., 2022). Furthermore, we explored the UV–vis absorption spectra of SH-CDs and SH-CDs@ACR (Fig. 4b). The investigation revealed that the introduction of ACR to the SH-CDs probe system resulted in a slight red shift in the absorption peak of the mixture, accompanied by a decrease in peak broadening. These observations suggested a chemical interaction between SH-CDs and ACR, leading to the formation of an unexpected compound (Dai & Hu, 2022). Next, the transient fluorescence spectra were recorded before and after introducing ACR into SH-CDs (Fig. 4c). The fluorescence lifetime of SH-CDs (4.78 ns) exhibited negligible variation upon addition of ACR (4.66 ns), indicating the quenching effect of SH-CDs by ACR may be attributed to SQE (Jia et al., 2019; Qin et al., 2020).

Additionally, the presence of a SQE between SH-CDs and ACR was further confirmed through theory calculations. In this study, the inner core of the SH-CDs was simulated using four polymerized benzene rings, and the optimal structure models of SH-CDs and SH-CDs@ACR were depicted in Fig. S4. The photophysical characteristics of SH-CDs and their reaction product with ACR were investigated via density functional theory (DFT) employing the B3LYP/6-31G (d,p) level with the Gaussian 16 package (Y. Xu, Wang, et al., 2021). The calculated fluorescence spectra were in good agreement with the aforementioned experiment results (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. 4d, the implementation of ACR did not lead to any alteration in the distribution of electron cloud density within the HOMO of the novel complex when compared to SH-CDs. Moreover, the energy levels of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) in SH-CDs were determined to be −1.34 eV and −6.26 eV, whereas the energy levels of HOMO and LUMO in SH-CDs@ACR were shifted to −2.35 eV and −6.77 eV. The energy levels of SH-CDs@ACR and corresponding energy gaps were lower than that of SH-CDs, which further demonstrated the occurrence of SQE. Unlike FRET and IFE, which require significant spectral overlap for energy transfer, the SQE involves direct chemical complex formation between SH-CDs and ACR. This interaction provides high selectivity and reduced sensitivity to environmental factors such as solvent polarity and pH, resulting in greater robustness and stability under varying conditions. These characteristics make SQE well-suited for practical applications, as it remains stable even under varying environmental conditions. This contributes to the high consistency and reproducibility observed in our dual-mode detection system, enhancing its potential for practical applications.

The performance of the dual-mode probe was further assessed in terms of its selectivity and resistance to interference. The selectivity of SH-CDs dual-mode probe was assessed by examining the effects of structural analogs. Surprisingly, only the addition of ACR induced distinct changes in both fluorescence and absorbance intensities, while the presence of other analogs had a negligible impact on the dual-mode probe under identical detection conditions (Fig. 5 a and b). It was suggested that the probe we proposed exhibited a high level of specificity towards ACR. Additionally, the anti-interference properties of the dual-mode sensor were examined in the presence of potential interfering substances, including cation ions (Fe³⁺, Na⁺, Ca2⁺, K⁺), anions (PO₄³⁻, Cl⁻, SO₄2⁻), as well as amino acids, fatty acids, and other small molecules (Glu, Ser, OA, LA, α-Toc, β-Car), both with and without ACR. There was no observable change in both fluorescence and absorbance intensities upon the introduction of common the interfering substances (Fig. 5c and d). However, the introduction of ACR resulted in notable changes in the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of the detection system, indicating that the probe exhibited remarkable anti-interference properties for ACR detection. The SH-CDs dual-mode probe demonstrated excellent selectivity and anti-interference in the presence of interferences within a complex environment, thereby highlighting its potential for application in complex circumstance. Additionally, the sensing performance of the dual-mode sensor was evaluated daily for a month to confirm its detection stability. The continuous detection of ACR over a period of one month, as depicted in Fig. S6, exhibited negligible changes in absorbance and fluorescence intensity. The observations indicate the exceptional detection stability of this dual-mode probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ACR based on smartphone-sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Given the inherent limitations of human visual perception in detecting subtle differences in probe color, it is not feasible to accurately determine the ACR content solely through visual observation. Meanwhile, in response to the demand for on-site ACR detection in practical applications, a portable sensing device was developed utilizing smartphone-integrated SH-CDs and a UV dark box. The smartphone-based detection approach, illustrated in Fig. 6 a, involved a three-step process: sample preparation, image capture, and data analysis. The centrifuge tube containing the dual-mode probe was added to varying concentrations of ACR. After 40 min, the solution was transferred to a cuvette, and the fluorescent or colorimetric images were captured by a smartphone camera in a dark box (Fig. S7). Ultimately, the image color was transformed into numerical values of the RGB color model by the Color Picker software, facilitating the quantitative detection of ACR. As depicted in Fig. 6b, the system image captured by the smartphone exhibited a noticeable yellow attenuation and turned from pale yellow to deep gray as the ACR concentration increased from 1 to 60 μМ under UV lamps and sunlight, respectively. After analyzing the RGB value, a significant correlation was found between the natural logarithm of ACR concentration and the R/(R + G + B) ratio of fluorescence images (y = −0.0354x + 0.5122, R2 = 0.987) within the range of 1–60 μМ (Fig. 6c). The linear equation between the logarithm of ACR concentration and the green channel value (G) of colorimetry images was y = −21.762x + 234.95, with an R2 value of 0.982 (Fig. 6d). These correlations demonstrated a remarkable resemblance to the observed trend from fluorescence spectrometer and UV–vis spectrometer, respectively. The result suggested that the smartphone assisted analyzer has the potential to offer a rapid and cost-effective quantitative determination of ACR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical detection efficiency of the SH-CDs, representative samples such as peanut oil and corn oil were analyzed. The oil samples that underwent pre-treatment did not show any detectable presence of ACR, which led to the need for implementing the standard addition technique at three different levels of concentration (0.5, 5, and 20 μМ). The average recovery and its relative standard deviation (RSD) were determined based on triplicate measurements conducted at various concentrations. As shown in Table 1 , the dua-mode probe exhibited satisfactory recoveries (94.0–112.0%) and low RSDs (1.3–3.2%) for both oil sample categories. Simultaneously, the reliability of this intelligent device for quantitative detection of ACR was evaluated by recording the corresponding digital values associated with the colors of fluorescence and solution. The range of recoveries observed was between 92.2% and 112.85% (Table S2), suggesting the accuracy of the smart device for determination of ACR in real food samples. In general, the proposed approach exhibited favorable outcomes in the detection of ACR in real samples, thereby affirming its practicality and dependability.

In this study, we have successfully developed and validated a high-efficiency fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode probe SH-CDs for the detection of ACR. Owing to the functionalization of sulfhydryl groups in SH-CDs, the constructed sensing system demonstrated remarkable resistance to interference and displayed high selectivity. The presence of ACR resulted in a decrease in the yellow fluorescence intensity of SH-CDs due to static quenching effect, while the solution generated a noticeable gray color under sunlight. Benefiting from the Michael addition reaction and dual signal output, we have successfully achieved a highly sensitive analysis for ACR with a broad detection range and an exceptionally low limit of detection. Meanwhile, satisfactory results were obtained from spiked recovery experiments, demonstrating the dual-mode capability of the sensor in quantifying ACR concentration in vegetable oil. In addition, the effective integration between the probe and a smartphone has enhanced its capability to visually determine ACR by converting color variations into digital value. Therefore, the proposed dual-mode sensing system provided a convenient, straightforward, and affordable approach for the real-time detection of ACR in food.
Jiangbin Xu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Wei Yang: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Yuanfa Liu: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFD2100300) and Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food (BM2020023) as well as Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor program.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fbio.2024.105586.
